post_2: small2015stabilitycorenets the article by mario small and colleagues conducted various studies and had incredible discoveries. for instance, the researchers discovered how one's routine helps develop new experiences and has an impact on whom one turns to for support. along with this, they saw how college students respond to new obligations and how ongoing routines add to the evidence that context matters to the structure of social support and networks. something i thought was interesting was how people respond quickly to changing circumstances and adapt by changing their discussion partners. the core discussion network is a major source of support throughout a person’s life, and it represents one of the most important ways that social networks are said to benefit everyday well-being. i would have assumed those who are not fond of change, would have more trouble adjusting to a new routine in their life. in a sense this is true, yet, when they have a social and reliable network, they can adapt more to the changing environment. something that i was a bit confused about was the concept of ego. for instance, ego-centrismism can be presented in various ways. however, does this indicate at a certain age we experience this? would this also be the case for children or elders perhaps? this ties in with the reading of last week by cohen and colleagues, as we see how a change in our social environment (specifically if it is during a stressful situation, like moving out), can affect our health if we don't have a strong support system during a time of need. perkinsetal2015 i liked this article by perkins, as it had an interesting perspective on the social networks in low- and middle-income countries (lmics). the article emphasized the formation of social ties, network structures, and function, and the associated mechanisms linking health or health behaviors, and how this may be relevant in the limited resources or targeting public health and economic development interventions in lmics. however, they were able to find how individuals in communities worldwide are linked together somehow through strong and weak ties representing many types of relationships. i was interested in the results of the interventions, as they can spread more positive than negative outcomes among individuals and communities in lmics. for instance, when they mentioned how a study of 19 slums in nepal demonstrated that an intervention providing access to savings accounts changed the network of financial transactions between two waves of data collection, i was surprised. we become stress-free of the financial burden when interventions allow individuals to make connections and can encourage the type of resources needed among a community that is suffering under these or various conditions. i was also interested in how the article mentioned the effects of hiv in a social context and how it was higher among people who were involved with incoming visitors. i was a bit confused with the concept of ‘change agent’, and wished the article elaborated on this more. this article ties in with the interventions in cohen’s work of week 3. after reading this article, i can now see how interventions can be provided efficiently in lmic communities, which serves as an influence for durkheim’s work with individuals who have experienced or thought of self-harming themselves.

post_1: what is one way in which these specific readings challenged your pre-existing beliefs about ios? what did you believe before versus what you read here? reading afghanistan´s significance for nato strategy by henrik larsen has challenged my pre-existing beliefs about the implication/importance of nato forces when it comes to building relationships with local communities. on page 45, larsen writes “it was hubris to expect young soldiers with deployments of 6–12 months, and with no language skills and little cultural understanding, to win the hearts and minds of afghan elders and to help build local governance and the rule of law”. my prior understanding of army forces was limited to combat operations. therefore, i had never appreciated the significance of building relationships with local communities. now, i know that military forces have a broader role to play in conflict zones beyond combat operations in order to achieve long-term stability and security in a territory. in conclusion, this reading has made me reconsider my assumptions about the role of army forces and the importance of a comprehensive, multi-dimensional approach that includes not only military operations, but also political, economic, and social measures aimed at addressing the underlying causes of instability. this can be something as simple as speaking to people in their own language.
post_2: this chapter was a very interesting and eye-opening read. something that greatly stood out to me throughout the reading was the talk of bias in relation to various categories, including race, gender, and socioeconomic status. the text describes bias as either intentional or unintentional, and it really made me reflect on my own biases. growing up in a homogeneous environment, i didn't realize how much my surroundings shaped my perceptions and judgments. however, despite my thinking that i am unbiased, i now recognize that unconscious bias can significantly affect my judgments and actions. due to this, i will be sure to work on becoming more aware of my biases and actively challenging them. this brings me to my question: do you think that travel and diverse experiences can effectively reduce bias, or is it more complex than that?

However, nowadays, it seems like more and more people are getting tattoos, and they are becoming much more accepted in society.

Can this trend imply a shift towards individualism in South Korea? What do you guys think? Is there something else at play here, or is this just one part of a larger cultural change?

post_2: the trobriander's do not have a traditional marriage ceremony. to make a marriage official, a woman must stay in her lover's hamlet past sunrise (which is when she usually leaves). in the morning, the couple waits for the woman's mother to bring them cooked yams. "if the girl's mother and mother's brother approve of her choice, her mother quickly cooks yams and carries them to her" (weiner 77). the marriage is officially recognized after the yams are eaten. afterward, the man's sister ties three skirts around her sister-in-law's waist and cuts them to the length of her knees. the woman's sexual freedom is gone and the partners remove their red shell necklaces to symbolize their union. if the marriage is not approved the woman's parents take their child home, sometimes by dragging her out if she resists. the woman can still marry her partner in secret regardless of her parents' demands and force them to accept it. a woman's mother is responsible for making decisions regarding her daughter's marriage. she decides whether or not the partner is acceptable, but cannot interfere until the marriage is announced. according to weiner, "if she disapproves of her daughter's choice, she argues that the boy is lazy and ugly" (77). a woman's father and brother are not allowed to have any discussions about her love affairs. however, the father can advise his son on whether or not his partner is suitable. if the father disapproves of his son's choice he will advise him against marrying her and suggest other candidates. fathers look for women who come from families with large political power and high social standing. they see marriages as an opportunity to create beneficial alliances with another family. my question is, what role does a mother play in her son's marriage?

post_1: what are the deeper meanings behind the diaper imagery? i don't know that this is a revelation that occurs during the reading as much as one i had after thinking about the text, but the cyclic nature of life and relationships really struck me. men watch their fathers age and die, then they themselves become fathers. fathers often regress mentally, almost like they're reverting back to infants. there is something in the emphasis on diaper imagery and infantilization that ties into this cycle. it's like the narrator says, "the old man is just a baby again," and i thought about how the transference of love from fathers to sons is so profound. and then immediately, the confusion about the purpose of the grandfather's scratching adds another layer of complexity. i know obviously that the scratching could be a sign of discomfort or restlessness, but it also seems to symbolize something deeper, maybe a struggle against the inevitable decline. there is something in the text about the cyclical nature of care and dependency, too. it’s like the roles reverse, and the sons who once needed their fathers now have to take care of them. question: what do you think the grandfather's scratching symbolizes in the context of this cycle?

post_1: the holy roman empire was created in 800 ad based on a deal made by charlemagne and pope leo ii i. after the collapse of the western roman empire, there were constant fighting to convert others to chritianity within western christendom. charlemagne sees this as an opportunity to claim the throne but also satisfy the traditions of the church. pope leo iii would give charlemagne the authority, while charlemagne acknowledged the church’s superiority over his power. (crash course) according to remote lecture #6, all leaders believe their duty is to prepare for the second coming of christ. emperor charles v became the ruler of most of western europe by the year 1519. charles’s parents came from 2 dynasties. from his maternal side, he laid claims to spain, parts of italy, and the americas. through his parental side, charles laid claim to the german lands in central europe. the holy roman empire was a fragmented collection of largely independent states and was divided between catholic and protestant rulership (western civilization). the protestant reformation, so-called “wars of religion” in europe, is a century-long war initially between the 2 religions then gradually developed into a continuation of the france-habsburg rivalry. martin luther’s beliefs of sola fide, only faith, rationalize that salvation could only be achieved by faith. this then comes in conflict with the catholic church when a friar was selling indulgences that allegedly reduce a sinner’s time in purgatory. in the 95 theses, the 2nd states “this word cannot be understood as referring to the sacrament of penance… as administered by the clergy” (the 95 theses). i agree with this policy that luther was criticizing because what he’s saying here is that salvation can only be given by god, not a priest. the reason why this is convincing to me because often in the past, you see people claiming themselves to have divine powers and thus others must obey them. certain priests abuse the people’s faith in order to gain power and wealth. what martin lither was doing is to offer a way for one to embrace their faith without having to pay for their sins. one question i’m left with is that isn’t it common for a skeptic to be executed, how is it that martin luther still alive at the time despite his rational thoughts against the church’s policies? works cited: mclean, john. “western civilization.” religious divide in the holy roman empire | western civilization, courses.lumenlearning.com/suny-hccc-worldhistory/chapter/religious-divide-in-the-holy-roman-empire/. martin luther's 95 theses, www.luther.de/en/95thesen.html. luther and the protestant reformation: crash course world history #218
